title: Podman socket does not create docker.sock for rootless Docker Compose
body:
Most of the time I work with root-less Podman and my projects work fine. For a while I have been using [Podman-Compose](https://github.com/containers/podman-compose), but recently switched to Docker Compose backed by Podman.  
In order for Docker Compose to work with rootless Podman, I have to manually symlink `/run/user/1002/podman/podman.sock` to `/var/run/docker.sock`. 
First, I enable podman socket (non-root). Then start it and then symlink it to /var/run/docker.sock:  
```
systemctl --user enable podman.socket
systemctl --user start podman.socket
sudo ln -s /run/user/1002/podman/podman.sock /var/run/docker.sock
```

After this I can run docker-compose root-less no problem! But, after restarting the system, I still see /var/run/docker.sock is not a symlink from /run/user/1002/podman.sock:  

>ls -la /var/run/docker.sock
>lrwxrwxrwx. 1 root root 23 Jun 24 05:37 /var/run/docker.sock -> /run/podman/podman.sock

I find this cumbersome. Is it possible to make `systemctl --user` automatically create the correct user-level (non-root) symlink so that docker-compose can work with it?  

Not sure this is a bug report or a feature request. I'd suppose the latter, though. 

**Is this a BUG REPORT or FEATURE REQUEST? (leave only one on its own line)**

/kind bug

/kind feature

**Description**

**Output of `podman version`:**

```
3.2.0
```

**Output of `podman info --debug`:**

comments.body:
I would say this up to the user to configure this. If you install `podman-docker` a symlink from `/run/docker.sock` to `/run/podman/podman.sock` is created. How should this work when several users activate the socket? You can only symlink to one destination. Also normal users should never get access to `/run/docker.sock`.

If you want your docker tools to talk to the rootless podman socket just set the `DOCKER_HOST` envar, e.g. `DOCKER_HOST=unix:///run/user/1002/podman/podman.sock`
I think `docker-compose` may also use the rootless Docker socket at `$XDG_RUNTIME_DIR/docker.sock` which we ought to be able to create ourselves. Needs further testing.

No they don't. Docker always uses `/run/docker.sock` even when you are rootless. They do this because they allow access to the socket for normal users when you are part of the docker group. Even for the docker rootless cli you have to set ´DOCKER_HOST`, see https://docs.docker.com/engine/security/rootless/#client

It looks like they do support this but only via contexts, which means explicit configuration of Docker is still required.

I do wonder if the context is writing a config file somewhere we can mock to set the default socket path.

hello, what is the temporal solution for this?, like @Luap99 said, i was trying to find the config file to use $XDG_RUNTIME_DIR/podman/docker.sock, but not luck and now podman not working.

i have a server that have installed podman since podman 3.0, and is currently on 3.2.2, and is working. (don't know how).

solution:

when /run/user/$IDE/podman/podman.sock is created.

do a ln -s /run/user/$ID/podman/podman.sock /run/user/$ID/docker.sock

- while for the user wait the fix, only need to do, delete the image and pull again (need test), or podman system prune -a.
ln -s /run/user/${id -u}/podman/podman.sock /run/user/${id -u}/docker.sock
Does setting the `DOCKER_HOST` environment variable to the path of the Podman socket not work?

> Does setting the `DOCKER_HOST` environment variable to the path of the Podman socket not work?

the normal user not have access to this folder.

> I'm asking whether setting the `DOCKER_HOST` environment variable to point to the _rootless_ Podman socket works?

no, no work.
> using the podman.sock of the user with DOCKER_HOST:
> ![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/7697566/125312581-68a4c280-e302-11eb-8a9f-407e2991e890.png)

**All this from the rootless user point view**

- export DOCKER_HOST=/var/run/podman/podman.sock (Permission denied)
- export DOCKER_HOST=/var/run/docker.sock


![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/7697566/125315817-7c055d00-e305-11eb-87b4-42942f89dcbd.png)

- export DOCKER_HOST=/run/docker.sock 
- export DOCKER_HOST=/run/podman/podman.sock (Permission denied)

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/7697566/125315616-4eb8af00-e305-11eb-8b6c-b6ea9fc9819c.png)

- export DOCKER_HOST=/run/user/20000/podman/podman.sock
- ln -s /run/user/20000/podman/podman.sock /run/user/20000/docker.sock
- export DOCKER_HOST=/run/user/20000/docker.sock 

Same for /var/run/docker.sock, /run/docker.sock, /run/user/20000/docker.sock:
![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/7697566/125316080-c4247f80-e305-11eb-9bc3-e55292357a79.png)

Same for /var/run/podman/podman.sock, /run/podman/podman.sock, /run/user/20000/podman/podman.sock
![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/7697566/125316426-1c5b8180-e306-11eb-92f8-b4ef6c06a910.png)

You need to prefix with `unix://` to indicate this is a path to a Unix socket. I would expect `DOCKER_HOST=unix:///run/user/$UID/podman/podman.sock` to work (with $UID replaced with the UID of the user)

> You need to prefix with `unix://` to indicate this is a path to a Unix socket. I would expect `DOCKER_HOST=unix:///run/user/$UID/podman/podman.sock` to work (with $UID replaced with the UID of the user)

This work, i am new on this.

Thanks @mheon
but podman, have to create this type of env, when enabling or running the systemd podman.socket
